[IMAGE:0]It's just a few minutes to midnight, so we can launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. (I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above on the left was my twentieth published book, The Ecotechnic Future. By this time my blog "The Archdruid Report" had been appearing weekly for a couple of years and was making an unexpected (to me) splash in the peak oil scene. Initial sales on my first peak oil book, The Long Descent, were good enough that the publisher was interested in a second volume, and of course I was perfectly willing to oblige. The Ecotechnic Future was my first attempt to put the decline and fall of industrial civilization into its broader context and talk about human history on the grand scale; to my mind, it stands up very well after all these years.

This was also, however, the first book of mine I had to put up a sustained fight to get through the editing process intac.  The editor wasn't satisfied with correcting my grammar and the like, which I'm fine with -- he tried to insist that I should rewrite the book to say what he thought it should say, not what I was trying to say. Maybe it was that I was contradicting the conventional wisdom too obviously, maybe it was something else, but for a decade or so from 2007 or so on, I had to deal with repeated attempts of this kind, and not just from one publisher, either. Fortunately I was able to push back hard, but it ended up causing me to change publishers more than once.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

Good Evening,

A very busy day for you, JMG! Thank you for sharing your multiple flavors and sources of insight. Also, to everyone who marks it, a very happy Easter!

To Share: 1. No long post this week, but I've typed up the expanded version of the Five Rites I shared back around the Winter Solstice, which I've picked back up in the past month or two after a bit of a hiatus. To my mind, they present some pretty compelling points, but I'm not 100% sold, and prayer and divination have led me to keep on worshiping Them separately in my own practice, but if nothing else, it's a treasure trove of comparative mythological material on Freyja, so if that sounds up your alley, check it out: https://www.spreaker.com/episode/is-freyja-the-same-goddess-as-eastre--19323457

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

comments.title: Are the retroshades really a thing?
comments.content:
As we head into Mercury retrograde proper, I thought I'd ask about an apparently-related phenomenon I've only head of within the last few years: the pre-retrograde and post-retrograde periods, also known as the "retroshades."

These are the periods of approximately two weeks before and after the retrograde which during Mercury is slowing down or speeding up while passing through the area that it did or will pass through going backwards during the retrograde.

From what I've read, these periods are supposed to be roughly similar to the retrograde, just less intense (or in the case of one article, more intense) and so it's best to preemptively avoid any activities that would be an issue during the retrograde, or to continue avoiding them for a couple weeks.

I'm wondering if this is a traditionally-recognized phenomenon or a modern notion? And also for both you and the gallery, what if anything your experience with these periods has been.

Retroshades? Perhaps like these?

[IMAGE:0]

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Oh, it can be done. Keep in mind, though, that entire Arthurian legendarium is a tale of failure -- of a bright dream dragged down to defeat by the personal flaws of the people who could have led it to victory. That dynamic inevitably plays out in Arthurian workings. I've seen a great many of them, and all the ones I've watched traced the same trajectory from bright hopeful beginnings to failure and regret.

It's possible to do effective workings with some of the legends that got caught up in the Arthurian legendarium; the Grail mythos is one example, the older legends of Merlin another. (Gray's books work with the former; my book The Mysteries of Merlin, soon to be out of print, is an example of the latter.) But the core of it -- Arthur, Camelot, the Round Table, etc. -- is best left unworked because of its essential dynamic.

replies.title: Arthurian
replies.content:
A friend of mine wrote an academic paper comparing the success of the film _Braveheart_, which has more to do with the ballad by Blind Harry than with actual history, and the dismal film, _First Knight_, with Sean Connery as Arthur and Richard Gere sleepwalking his way through the role of Lancelot. She noted that _First Knight_ moved away from the traditional legend, giving it a "happy ending" as Arthur forgives Genièvre and Lancelot for their betrayal of him, dies, and leaves them the kingdom. Her premise, as I recall it, was that the legends has stood the test of time--they tell the story the audience wants (one might even say, needs) and that the happy ending for Lance and Genivieve violated what the audience knew was the proper ending. Of course, the film had many other faults, so hard to say what actually caused its failure (46th grossing that year.)

I'm surmising that from an occult point of view, legends tell us what we need to hear not necessarily what we want to hear.

Rita

replies.title: Re: Arthurian
replies.content: It's a little more than that. From an occult point of view, legends express truths rather than facts -- that is, they describe things that never happened but always are. If we want to hear them, they're popular; if we need to hear them, they're loudly denounced; if they're more or less irrelevant to our present wants and needs, they slumber in old books and the memories of storytellers until they're needed again. What makes retconning like First Knight ring so false isn't simply that everyone knew the real ending; it's that the retconned ending is a lie -- it abandons the truths that the legend is there to enshrine and express.

I've recently done an IQ test, and apparently have an IQ of just over 180. My IQ is high enough that fewer than 1 in a million people have this sort of intellectual capacity. This also matches my own experience. When I was 8, I decided to spend a summer learning quantum physics; my parents found it disturbing when I actually succeeded; even though it took me closer to a year, I was eventually able to actually discuss the finer points of it, and explain why I found it implausible in a way that showed them (an engineer and a professor) that I actually knew my way around it enough to have novel insights, before I turned 10.

This has been much more of a curse than a blessing: there are very, very few people I actually enjoy talking to, because most people are, from my perspective, idiots, at best; most books, TV shows, movies, nearly all forms of culture of any sort are trite and predictable; and I can't even use my freak intelligence very well, because I struggle to dumb down my ideas enough that most people can understand what I'm trying to say.

Part of the problem, if I'm being honest, is also that as much as I have this freak intelligence my emotional and practical sides are poorly developed at best; and so while I have an absurd gift, it's paired with major deficits in other parts of my life, which just makes the entire thing even worse.

I have next to no occult knowledge, but have started looking into the field, since I've started practicing using Leaning Ritual Magic and am interested in studying as much as possible.

a) I can't currently remember any of my prior lives, but I assume that this likely means I spent a long time overdeveloping my logical side and neglecting other aspects of being human. Does this sound plausible to you?

b) Is there a good way to make sure that my future lives do not suffer from this curse? I assume working on other aspects of my life will help, but do you have advice for bringing my intelligence in future lives down to a more normal level?

replies.content:
Not JMG, and not part of that club, but a few ideas to take or leave as you like:

1) Consider picking up some things that require skill and practice, but not raw intellectual horsepower. Handcrafts are good for this, physical skills like lifting weights or martial arts might be even better. These tend to do a very good job of highlighting the difference between getting something conceptually and actually bringing it into material manifestation.

2) Related: consider locking yourself into a commitment that emphasize other areas of yourself, and where you are likely to be surrounded by folks who are less intellectually focused. I joined the Army and became an airborne infantryman. I don't necessarily recommend such an extreme approach, but it taught me that there's a big difference between having intellectual horsepower (IQ) or not and having a willingness and interest to learn. I met a lot of guys who were pretty average intellectually, but were curious and wanted to learn about the world, and I found I could get along with them much better than more intelligent people who are locked into particular forms of knowledge.

3) I wouldn't say to seek these out, but some life experiences make excellent fodder for this kind of thing. Like grief. You can understand your own grief, why people grieve, what the grieving process looks like, how it will be when you've gone through it, and so forth, but you also still have to go through it. This has been a (hopefully useful) challenge for me.

4) Pay attention to the areas of your life where you "know better," but still do things otherwise. Maybe you intellectually get that having these boring conversations would grease certain social wheels and open certain opportunities to you, but you just can't make yourself care. Or maybe you know that eating candy is bad for your teeth and your long term health, but it's right there. Or whatever. These can be windows into the stuff that's motivating you besides your intellect, and the power those things can have, even in the face of overwhelming rational arguments. Once you recognize those drives/forces are there, you can try to cultivate a more aware and helpful engagement with them (or so it seems - I'm still on very early steps with a lot of this!)

At any rate, hope some of the above helps, and good luck,
Jeff

The first time he came home reporting that he met so-and-so who knew me and who said Hi and, by the way I was the smartest person ever at that company, I was charmed. replies.title: Some thoughts from a less lofty intellect
replies.content:
Thank you for sharing your experience to open this compelling discussion. Mine was tested in the 140s in my youth. I think that's accurate, as in almost every situation of my life, I've been in the top percentile intellectually. Thus I can sympathize from having a lesser version of the problem.

Some ideas for your consideration:

1 A great deal of valid information about life comes from the physical body, and from intuition. Both provide information that can be analyzed once it arrives, but intellectual acuity has nothing to do with awareness and openness to these sources of information.

2 A great deal of valid enjoyment in life comes from using the body and the emotions, from enjoying presence with others and shared activities. These do not require peer to peer intellectual capacity to be meaningful, happy, fulfilling moments. In the same way a 7 foot tall person might like to find other giants with whom to play basketball, while otherwise adapting to the average heights of those around them, you might like to find people with extraordinary brilliance on topics that interest you and strike up conversations and correspondence; or, as JMG does, write, publish, speak and be interviewed as an expert, without attempting to dumb your ideas down for the masses. If your abundant springs of intellect can be put into constructive channels, they might be less at risk of massive flooding of the plains.

3 Great intellect is like being able to see a million more shades of blue than others can discern. Perhaps useful for a painter or weatherman, but this says nothing about whether greenery or sunsets could be experienced and valued the same as for anyone else. In what ways are your ordinary? In what ways can you learn from others, with a definition of learning not limited to intellectual symbol manipulation?

4 My parents were in a cult based entirely on abstract concepts about God, with intellectual agreement with the doctrine said to be life's greatest goal. (It was a corrupt spinoff of Transcendentalism.) My childhood was full of indoctrination which said emotions and physical experience were irrelevant, the body was shunned, the material world was a gnostic temptation. While I rejected the doctrine, I now believe this led me to waste enormous amounts of time seeking The One True Systematic Answer in my spiritual search, rather than seeking a relationship with All That Is. I now find meditation, intuition, and simply being present with nature increasingly important to me. JMG has a huge wealth of material about magical traditions which involve visualization, emotional balance, relationship, divination, meditation, all of which are ways of knowing other than instant abundant cross-referencing of syllogisms.

5 I found the Michael Teachings system of spirituality helpful. It includes a cosmology and an approach to personality types such as goals and attitudes. You might find it useful to look at their discussions of the Scholar archetype and how its facets can be included or not included in an individual. Here is a brief overview that will help you decide if you'd like to learn more. (Note that there are now multiple sources of this information with slight variations. One thing they agree on is that the teachings are not a command to have faith, but an invitation to evaluate whether or not its elements prove useful to your own life.)
https://itstime.com/game.htm

I hope these provide some useful food for thought.

Mocha Amphibious Moose

I didn't see this until pretty late, so I'm not writing this quite as well as I would want, but here's my two cents:

One option you may not have been aware of, and that you might have comparative advantage at, is getting your emotional thinking and your logical thinking more into more of a single unified and commensuration-enabling conceptual language, using ideas from the various sciences of decision making. I would start by learning the chain of prerequisites leading up to that body of theory which engineers call "optimal stochastic control", operations research practitioners call "optimal sequential decision theory", and computer scientists call "partially observable Markov decision processes".

For this to be more than just slightly helpful, you would also need to learn some intuitions and metaphors from complex systems sciences, such as economics, statistical physics, and perhaps ecology. You would also need a beginning understanding of the statistical-inference concept of "factor loading sparsity priors" or "relevance determination". At that point, you would have the pieces needed to understand how you would design a system to do a few of the things that some of your simplest unconscious emotional response patterns and habits are doing, and start slotting them into an understanding of what a correctly organized broader system would look like. I think of this sort of like a form of tikkun olam, and a tiny shard of the Great Work.

There's a whole space that opens out from there. Though you may be too old already to get much use out of it. Also there's always a temptation to pretend your intellectual representation of how a motivation does/should work can stand and substitute for being in touch with the actual state and workings of the motivation itself.

I don't have good recommendations for books about these ideas, but here are mediocre recommendations:

- "Algorithms To Live By", by Brian Christian and Tom Griffiths. The book isn't all that good by my standards, since it's far away from the math. Griffiths's primary research deals in a lot more interesting and relevant stuff, but it's less accessible.

- "The Hidden Pattern", by Ben Goertzel. This doesn't get into decision-theoretic foundations as much as it should, and I disapprove of Goertzel's intellectual excesses, but it does help convey a bit better the headspace in which it's possible to make a marriage of logical ideas about applied math intuitions (especially statistical physics) and the introspective experience of the workings of a mind. It also makes some helpful Kabbalistic-like connections to cosmology.

- "What is Mind?", by Eric Baum. This is less about the math and more about just orienting to the idea of bridging between applied-math-like descriptions and psychological descriptions at all.

- You might eventually get to the point where you would profit from checking out chapter 6 of Koller and Friedman's "Probabilistic Graphical Models".

- I found Douglas Hofstadter's "Metamagical Themas" helpful, but that's even further from the math. Your mileage may vary.

There are also various materials produced by the Yudkowsky-Alexander rationality movement, but I feel self-conscious about my moral hazard in recommending any of that, and at any rate they don't really talk about the underlying math nearly enough.

You may also be interested in the book "Aristoi", by Walter John Williams, which is a fictional but interestingly half-plausible exploration of some mental disciplines available at sufficiently high intelligence. It's not directly spelled out in the book, but there's an implication that the Aristoi get their fictional abilities at engineering, the arts, and social manipulation from a kind of phase transition, where everything they learn refines their understanding of useful subskills and abstract connections with everything else they learn, including meta-skills of drawing correct and precise analogies, and it all catches fire together.

One element that comes up is that the Aristoi end up automatically finding it more efficient to cultivate of subpersonalities to divide up cognitive work, and the maintenance of right order among those subpersonalities. This isn't really very practical without knowing principles and tips for how that maintenance of right order would work. In the book this practical wisdom is fictional and mostly offscreen, and comes from the genius of the original Aristos and the hard-won experience of the inheriting Aristoi over most of a preceding millenium. However, it does highlight the point that, in the long run, you can't get much use out of putting your mind and your logical understanding in overlapping spaces, without getting a good understanding of properly managing conflict and negotiation between different aspects, decision heuristic pressures, and motivational factions within a mind. Here, an understanding of economics or public choice theory might be useful.

replies.title: mirrors
replies.content:
I have a theory that the superstitions around mirrors may be associated with the old theory that we see by projecting rays from our eyes. If this were the case an object able to catch those rays and send them back to us could seem to be perhaps capable of holding some of that energy, hanging on to part of everyone who ever looked in the mirror and breaking it could have negative consequences. I'm going on the assumption that our ancestors didn't make up stupid superstitions just for fun--that they are often part of forgotten systems of belief.

Rita

comments.title: SoP and Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,

Having recently completed the 8-week program for learning the Sphere of Protection, I am now considering advancing to the Opening and Closing of a FHR Temple. I have a question regarding this transition.

During the SoP, I typically stand at the center of my ritual space, facing the East. I commence with the Opening, invoke Air, then pivot to address the remaining three directions in sequence. Following this, I invoke Spirit Below, Spirit Above, and Spirit Within, and conclude with the Closing. Throughout this process, I remain stationary at the center of my ritual space.

In the context of performing the SoP within the Opening of a FHR Temple, am I correct in understanding that after the Opening I should depart from the center of my ritual space and physically move to each of the four quarters? If so, how should I invoke Spirit Below and Spirit Above? Upon returning to the center of my ritual space, should I proceed to trace the orange and purple circles below and above me as usual? Or would it be more appropriate to trace them beneath and above the altar, with Spirit Within arising from the fusion of both Currents at the level of the altar surface?

Thank you in advance for your advice.

replies.title: Re: SoP and Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose
replies.content: Good! Yes, you go to the four quarters of the temple space to draw the elemental figures, and then you draw the circles of Spirit at the altar. I trace the orange circle just above the altar top and visualize it descending into the ground beneath the temple; I then trace the purple circle in the air above the altar and visualize it ascending up into the air above the temple.

replies.title: Re: SoP and Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose
replies.content:
Thank you very much for your reply, Mr Greer.

One more question, if I may: is it appropriate to use different sets of deities in the SoP? So far I've been using a selection of Continental Celtic deities. For some reason, using the "mainstream" Judeo-Christian Divine Names might actually make sense in a FHR context, and the use of these Divine Names presents the advantage of being familiar to me from my previous SIRP practice. Is it okay to switch between different sets of Divine Names according to need, context or inspiration? Or is it preferable to choose one set of Divine Names and stick to it?

Again, thank you for your advice.

replies.title: Re: SoP and Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose
replies.content: That's one of those unexpectedly subtle questions. Yes, it's generally a good idea to find one set of names and work with those, but inevitably students of the system spend a while experimenting with different sets of names. It's a flexible ritual, and permits that.

comments.title: Shadow People
comments.content:
JMG

Good morning. Unfortunately I don’t have a particularly nice subject to ask about. Recently a friendly acquaintance of mine asked in a separate chat if someone had experience with the occult. Apparently his nephews are drawing pictures of 7-8 foot tall shadow creatures that they say they see in the door ways.

Background to this the Sister in Law recently got divorced from his brother and has custody of the children. Brother was very into drugs including psychedelics. He had been sneaking psylociban into her food as well as using the home security system to speak to her and the children as the voice of god. According to my acquaintance whatever spirits he was communing with encouraged rampant infidelity with all of the pregnancies/abortions that implies. Also the family is part Cherokee and to quote my friend “my brother is into all that Indian crap.”

The Acquaintance and I met via an anti-vax military chat. He is very much on the fundamentalist side of Christianity with all that implies. Also they live several states away so in person investigation or action is out of the question.

The sister in law has a history of using marijuana and alcohol but is clean now. The family is attending church more regularly. Earlier in this odyssey she believed that she had a demon attached to her but that it isn’t now. She has tried regular prayer and anointing the doorways with oil (unknown type) but that hasn’t worked.

I recommended that they remove all of the brothers possessions from the house, smudge the place with sage, then mop the floors with hoodoo mix as if they were moving into a new house, regular hoodoo baths, and some prayers to St. Michael. Also if they can go have some experiences of genuine childlike joy. Preferably in the house but I get why that could be tough.

I figure the majority of the problem is that the etheric and astral environments in the house is terrible. The above should take care of most of that. But because of their background, beliefs, and the separation something like a witch bottle or talisman is out of the question.

I was hoping you or the commitariat might have some other ideas that could be thrown at the problem keeping in mind the above constraints.

Also has anyone here dealt with a Shadow Person or Hagging problem before?

As an academic exercise if the above constraints weren’t an issue what else would you do? I was thinking adding the Rose Cross ritual and a talisman to the Archangel Michael.

Thanks again for your time and hosting this space.

Other Dave

replies.title: Re: Shadow People
replies.content:
Ouch. Yeah, that sounds like the sort of mess you get when drug use gets mixed with sleazy magic.

I have two suggestions. The first is that your suggestion of a Rose Cross ritual and an archangelic talisman is a good one. The other is to have the children, when they see one of the shadows in the doorways, throw dry beans at it. That's an old Greek and Roman trick, and it's one that I've known children to use very successfully against nasty energetic phenomena -- in one case, they did it completely spontaneously, without knowing a thing about the tradition.

replies.title: Re: Shadow People
replies.content: Thank you.  The bean thing I can probably get them to do.  Unfortunately they are four states away so the RC and talismans are out.  I doubt they would permit me to do that anyway.

replies.title: Re: Shadow People
replies.content: Ask them if you can pray for them; that's adequate consent in this kind of situation, since they don't understand that magic is in fact a kind of prayer. Then do the R+C ritual while visualizing yourself in the house, and concentrate on the divine white brilliance descending into the house and cleansing it of all evil forces. That kind of distance working can be surprisingly effective, especially if repeated -- say, six times to keep with the Tiphareth symbolism.

comments.title: Pentacles in Catholic church windows
comments.content: If permitted, JMG, a question partly to Christian readers but also to solicit your opinion please: I've been going for some time now to a local Catholic mass which, from what I can see, really boils down to a magical ritual. The more I learn about the place of symbols and ritual in high magic, the more I recognise it in this Mass. The strange thing is, I have been to many churches in my life, of various stripes and denominations, and without exception I never felt what I feel in this little church. I have been conducting my own experiments to see if this was a "Catholic" thing, but my visits to other Catholic churches left me as cold as any stark protestant church I've ever been to. What gives? But the windows surrounding Mary are bare, save for an extraordinary profusion of pentacles. Not stars, honest to goodness pentacles. Is this usual in Catholic churches?

replies.title: Re: Pentacles in Catholic church windows
replies.content: Five-pointed stars in medieval Catholicism represented the five joys of Mary (there were five of those originally, before it became seven). Is it a very old church?

replies.title: Re: Pentacles in Catholic church windows
replies.content:
No, probably one of the youngest church buildings in my
town, the church dates back to Victorian times whereas the town dates back to Roman time and possibly earlier. There's something really odd about this church... I'll go read up about the five joys of Mary, thank you.

replies.title: Re: Pentacles in Catholic church windows
replies.content: In that case, I'd wonder if there had been something else on the site before -- or if the priest who celebrates the Mass is unusually capable.

replies.title: Re: Pentacles in Catholic church windows
replies.content: If you haven't already read it, I recommend "The Magic of Catholicism: Real Magic for Devout Catholics" by Brother A.D.A (also known as Agostino Taumaturgo, or his birth name, Jason Spadafore).  He's an independent Catholic bishop, with some background in traditionalist Catholicism.  He discusses this kind of thing (the use of pentacles/pentagram) in Roman Catholicism.  Catholicism has a rich history of magic, even if the modern Church skews heavily toward either (depending on the cleric or layperson) modernist and fundamentalist dismissals of magic.

replies.title: Re: Pentacles in Catholic church windows

If you want a very solid occult program that nonetheless is fairly traditionally Christian, you can hardly do better than his material. comments.title: Karma and climate change
comments.content:
I have been reflecting on karma and climate change. I see climate change, from raised CO2 levels, as a collective karma of industrial civilisation, one that many future civilisations will have to live with. What bothered me was that if its our task, if we want to finish incarnation, to clear up all our karma and develop our mental body, then wouldn't our ecological karma bind us to the planet?

If this were the case it would be a double bind as the human population will be dropping, leaving way less bodies for souls to populate. It'll also be at least a few thousand years at least to address the elevated CO2. That could mean a whole bunch of us reincarnation as animals.

I had also thought that there is a collective component and an individual component to it. There's plenty of us getting on with living simpler lives that are more gentle on the planet (including my family), but, a bit like a war, we are bound to a certain sense to an industrial system that we have limited control over (apart from gradually opting out).

So I saw the karma situation as similar to a war, if we accept our fate, don't exacerbate the problem, the physical side of the climate karma won't necessarily bind us here for future physical incarnations.

However if we merrily go on our ways as if nothing is a problem, it might.

As karma applies on the inner planes, I suspect what might happen is a lot of humans have to deal with the ecological karma on the etheric level instead. That wouldn't break the rules.

Am I on the right track?

replies.title: Re: Karma and climate change
replies.content:
Collective karma is not the same as individual karma. Each nation, culture, and civilization has its own group soul, which undergoes karmic transformations; individuals suffer the consequences of those as part of their own personal karma. Since no one person is responsible for more than a minute share of the problem, that can be dealt with in the ordinary way; I suspect many people are getting their karmic payback right now as the economy goes south and so many other things go wrong.

Meanwhile, industrial civilization's group soul is heading into its own brutal karmic blowback, which will leave very little of it standing. The process by which it goes to bits will give many other people a chance to work off the relevant karma.

replies.title: Re: Karma and climate change

replies.title: Re: For the very first time, I FELT hugged. I could feel it. Inside.
replies.content:
I've had something of a similar, though apparently less intense, experience - it was only a few years ago that I realized folks weren't being metaphorical when they called emotions "feelings."

For me, occult practice (I am working through JMG's Druid Magic Handbook and The Dolmen Arch) has helped here, with some meditation leading to very strong emotional reactions. Also, some of the stuff I mentioned upthread on the topic of "getting out of your head and into your body" might be relevant: https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/273418.html?thread=47496202#cmt47496202

As for further places to look: a term that might help you find some further reading is "dissociation" - in its extreme forms, folks become numb to most of what their body is doing, not only physical feelings associated with emotions. Often, it is a response to some kind of trauma, but not always. Tim Ferriss recounts the abuse he suffered that led to some of this, and ways he has dealt with it in a lengthy podcast with a therapist, and the page for it has lots of associated reading (mostly focused on trauma, which may or may not be relevant in your case, don't want to jump to conclusions, but the resources might be helpful regardless): https://tim.blog/2020/09/14/how-to-heal-trauma/

Some other relevant terms are "proprioception" and "somatic awareness" - there are various exercises for working on these, most of which I've found not incredibly helpful, but some folks report very strong responses. One that comes to mind is an all-body "scan," which is similar to some of the progressive relaxation techniques JMG teaches, as in the Modern Order of Essenes material, but followed up with just paying attention to any parts that are numb, tight, or unresponsive. I got this from Finding Your Own North Star, a self-help/coaching book that I now view as watered-down occultism, but does have a few practical exercises.

Other than that, finding ways to learn to pay attention to your body can be good - certain kinds of meditation or relaxation exercises can help, regular exercise (especially anything that involves a postural element, like yoga), cold/hot exposure, and so forth. Once you have a few things you know how to pay attention to, you can start trying to link felt-feelings with what's going on emotionally/cognitively, and figure out where they influence each other or get mistaken for each other (anxious, or just need to eat? stressed out, or just too much caffeine?).

Hope some of this helps, and good luck!
Jeff

replies.title: Finding Your Own North Star
replies.content:
Finding Your Own North Star - Jeff, would you recommend something better than this book that you've found since then? Is the book still valuable?

Christopher from California

replies.title: Re: Finding Your Own North Star
replies.content:
Eehhhh, I don't think it's not valuable, it's more that the amount of value in the book pales compared to actual occultism/magic books. It's also one of those books that might be exactly what some folks need, but could be bad for others - it focuses a lot on figuring out what you truly want and organizing your life to go after that and not being held back by self-defeating thoughts, inappropriate deference to others, and so forth. For someone who needs to be more assertive and self-directed, that might be great advice! For someone who needs to rein in his ego and focus more on the needs of others, it could be exactly wrong.

As for what to recommend instead, I have found scrying one of the most useful and affecting parts of my magical practice, when it comes to dealing with the parts of myself I haven't been as comfortable with - both scrying the Elemental Gates used in the SOP and some of the fews of the Ogham. For whatever reason, so far I've had an easier time connecting with such things through imaginal means than through my body.

comments.title: C-PTSD technique + magic
comments.content:
I have C-PTSD and found a technique for neurological dysregulation that feels to me like a magical act.

Twice a day, you write down your fears and resentments. At the end, you offer it up to a higher power and ask for it to be removed. After writing the dedication, rip up the paper.

Then you sit and chill for twenty minutes. Listen to a soothing sound, just relax and let the stress come down. (This is the Daily Practice by Ana Runkel.)

This has helped me to be consistently calmer in the last month than I ever remember being. I have even re-regulated myself some, with my brain coming down naturally.

I am wondering if this technique could be made even more potent as a mage if I view it as a magical act. (I'm Grade 6 of DA.)

Certainly when you rip up the paper after offering it to your higher power, that feels magical, destroying the fear and letting my HGA wipe it away.

Thanks for your time and thoughts.

replies.title: Re: C-PTSD technique + magic
replies.content:
Good heavens. That's very similar to an occult technique taught by Harry Gardener, one of the forgotten teachers I've been studying closely. The only difference is that he had his students burn the paper rather than ripping it up -- and ripping it up ought to do just as well.

Yes, it's a magical action. Any symbolic act done with intention and imagination is a magical action. When you've worked through the material on mentalism in the Gwyddon Rhydd grade, you ought to be able to integrate some of those principles in it if you want -- but even as it is, it's a very useful practice.

replies.title: Re: C-PTSD technique + magic

Ana learned it 30 years ago from a previously homeless woman who was going around the underbelly of San Francisco and teaching it to people whose lives had become smoking craters. Ana thinks it originated from the 12-step program because this woman was a mentor, so maybe there is overlap with Harry Gardener there.

3) God is not a vending machine. If you make yourself a vessel for divine power, that power works according to divine intention, not according to your desires: "Not my will, but Thine be done." So it's pretty much a matter of human thought, which is why there's so much you can't accomplish with it. As for the limits, there are quite a few of them: the laws of nature (you're not going to be able to make yourself float through the air no matter how frantically you try to "manifest" that), the law of karma (by and large, you will get what you deserve, not what you think you ought to deserve), and the fact that millions of other people are also using these same methods to try to get what they want, tolerably often at your expense, are among the most important.

replies.title: Re: Prison tai chi and limits to magical exercises

comments.title: Your Anti Theft Spell
comments.content:
Just a question that popped up while telling my wife about the anti-theft spell you put on the books you publish. Does it apply to copies bought at used bookstores? I haven't bought any of your books that way, but the thought did arise that someone might.

replies.title: Re: Your Anti Theft Spell
replies.content: Good heavens, no. If you buy it legally -- new, used, it doesn't matter -- you're fine. Used book stores benefit authors indirectly -- if there are steady sales of used copies, especially if the price ratchets upwards, that can convince publishers to bring out of print books back into print. Besides, back when I was a lot poorer than I am now, I used to buy most of my books at used book stores because that was what I could afford; I'm not going to turn around and close that door to others.

comments.title: Human souls
comments.content:
Hi JMG and All,
I wanted to ask your opinion on the total number of human souls. The number referenced in the Old Testament is 144 million human souls, finitely. Coincidentally the number 44 in old Hebrew is a term used for a really large number, what we would call today a “gazillion”, so not really a precise number but rather a wast unspecified quantity.
I had some interesting conversations with my daughter about this topic, and we decided to ask tarot about it. She has been reading tarot pretty successfully and I’m also a practitioner for many years.
Tarot gave an answer along these lines: initially there were unlimited and ever growing number of human souls but something happened due to human nature, something due to human desire to always wanting more ( the greed of wants) and some souls died or had been destroyed. A covenant was offered and a divine person started making souls again to bring the balance back and it’s still happening as of now. And tarot was adamant that indeed there’s about 144 million human souls only.
Do you know how many human souls there are?

replies.title: Re: Human souls
replies.content: In the traditions I follow, there isn't a fixed number. Being human is a particular stage in the process of spiritual evolution; there are always souls finishing up their human lives and going on to higher and more interesting states of being, and there are always souls who have just finished their animal incarnations and are starting the adventure of being human for the first time. Right now there are many more than 144 million, since there are more than 8 billion human bodies, and most of them are inhabited by souls that are more or less at the human level.
